title: Welcome
text:
This is a companion for Deep Learning with Python (3e) by François Chollet and Matthew Watson (copyright 2025).
Each chapter title to the left is a link to a slide deck.

These slides are being developed by this club.
Each deck will open in its own tab.
You may want to type “s” at the start of each deck to open the speaker notes.
Join the Data Science Learning Community to participate in the discussion!

We follow the Data Science Learning Community Code of Conduct.

title: 5. Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Fundamentals of machine learning













No matching items

title: 1. What is deep learning?
text:
What is deep learning?













No matching items

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Learning objectives

Understand the tension between optimization and generalization
Choose appropriate model evaluation strategies
Learn some regularization techniques to improve generalization

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Optimization vs generalization
The fundamental tension in machine learning:

Optimization: Adjusting the model to perform well on training data
Generalization: How well the model performs on data it has never seen


These two goals are at odds — and managing this tension is what ML is all about.



Early in training, both improve together (underfitting regime)
After a point, training loss keeps falling but validation loss plateaus or rises
That inflection point is where overfitting begins

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Underfitting vs overfitting



Canonical overfitting behavior.



Both curves drop together (underfitting), then diverge (overfitting)
The “robust fit” point is where we want to stop
This pattern is universal across all model types and datasets



The goal is to find and stop at the sweet spot
We’ll see tools for this: early stopping, regularization
The key diagnostic: compare training vs validation metrics

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Why models overfit
Three main sources:

Noisy training data — mislabeled samples or invalid inputs cause memorization of outliers
Ambiguous features — input regions that map to multiple classes; model becomes overconfident
Rare features → spurious correlations — uncommon values create false associations



MNIST experiment in the book: adding 784 white noise channels degraded validation accuracy by ~1%
The noise channels had no information, but the model found accidental patterns
Shuffled-label experiment: model memorized random labels perfectly — zero generalization
NOTEBOOK DEMO: Open notebooks/chapter05_demo.ipynb, Demo 1. Run the noise channels experiment and show the validation accuracy plot. Ask: “Why can’t the model just ignore the noise?”

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
The manifold hypothesis

Real-world data lives on low-dimensional manifolds within high-dimensional space
These manifolds are smooth and continuous — you can morph one sample into another




Manifold interpolation produces valid digits; linear interpolation does not.



Generalization via interpolation



Example: images of faces form a manifold — you can smoothly transition between faces
Not all points in pixel space are valid faces; valid ones cluster on a subspace
Gradient descent’s smooth, incremental nature helps capture manifold structure
“Training data is paramount” — you need dense sampling near decision boundaries

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Evaluating models: The three sets








Set
Purpose
When used




Training
Fit model parameters
Every epoch


Validation
Tune hyperparameters, detect overfitting
During development


Test
Final unbiased evaluation
Once, at the very end




Never tune anything based on test set performance
Information “leaks” from validation set into the model via your decisions



Every time you use validation performance to make a decision, information leaks
The test set must remain truly untouched until final evaluation
In Kaggle: validation = your local eval, test = the leaderboard

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Three evaluation protocols
1. Simple hold-out: Fixed train/val/test split

Fast, but unreliable with small datasets

2. K-fold cross-validation: Split data into K parts, rotate validation set

More reliable; average K results

3. Iterated K-fold with shuffling: Repeat K-fold P times, reshuffle each time

Most reliable; costs P × K model trainings



K=4 or K=5 is common
Iterated K-fold is gold standard for small datasets
For large datasets, simple hold-out is usually fine

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Evaluation pitfalls

Data representativeness: Always shuffle before splitting
Temporal ordering: For time-series, validate on future data only
Redundancy: Ensure no overlap between train and validation (e.g., duplicate photos)
Baseline: Always beat a common-sense baseline before celebrating



Temporal leaks are a classic mistake: training on future data to predict the past
Redundancy: if the same patient appears in train and val, you’re testing memorization
Common-sense baseline examples: majority-class classifier, last-value predictor

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Improving model fit
Three problems you’ll encounter:
1. Training loss doesn’t decrease

Learning rate too high (overshooting) or too low (stalling)

2. Training works but model doesn’t generalize

Data may lack predictive information, or architecture is wrong

3. Can’t achieve overfitting

Model is too small — increase layers or layer sizes



Achieving overfitting first is a key milestone — it proves your pipeline works
If you can’t overfit, the problem is capacity, not regularization
Architecture choice matters: use convnets for images, recurrent/transformers for sequences

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Improving generalization: Data

Collect more data — the single most effective thing you can do
Minimize labeling errors — garbage in, garbage out
Clean your data — handle missing values, remove duplicates
Feature selection — drop irrelevant features that add noise



Dense sampling of the input space is what enables interpolation
More data is almost always better than a fancier model
Cleaning data is unglamorous but high-impact

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Feature engineering
Applying domain knowledge to create better input representations:

Reading a clock: raw pixels → polar coordinates of the hands → time
Deep learning reduces the need, but doesn’t eliminate it

Good features still help because they:

Let you solve problems with less data
Enable simpler, more elegant models



Before deep learning, feature engineering was ~90% of the work
Deep learning automates much of it, but domain knowledge still helps
Example: for house prices, “price per square foot” > raw price + raw area

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Early stopping
Interrupt training when validation metrics stop improving: (We will learn this in chapter 7)
callbacks_list = [
    keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(
        monitor="val_loss",
        patience=2,
    ),
    keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint(
        filepath="best_model.keras",
        monitor="val_loss",
        save_best_only=True,
    )
]

patience: how many epochs to wait before stopping
ModelCheckpoint: saves the best model automatically



This is the simplest and most effective regularization technique
Always use both callbacks together — early stopping + checkpoint
The “best” model is from the epoch with lowest validation loss, not the last epoch

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Regularization: Reducing model size

Fewer parameters = fewer things to memorize
Forces the model to learn compressed, generalizable representations




Model
Behavior




Too small
Underfits — can’t capture the patterns


Too large
Overfits — memorizes training data


Just right
Generalizes well




Start small, increase capacity until overfitting, then regularize



There’s no formula for the right size — it’s empirical
The book’s heuristic: start with a model that overfits, then shrink/regularize
“Occam’s razor” — simplest model that fits the data
NOTEBOOK DEMO: Open notebooks/chapter05_demo.ipynb, Demo 2. Run the IMDB baseline (16-unit) and larger (512-unit) models. Plot both to show how the bigger model overfits much worse. (Pre-run these if short on time.)

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Regularization: Weight regularization
Add a penalty to the loss for large weight values:

L1: Penalty ∝ |w| — drives weights to exactly zero (sparse)
L2 (weight decay): Penalty ∝ w² — drives weights toward zero (small)

layers.Dense(16, activation="relu",
             kernel_regularizer=regularizers.l2(0.002))

0.002 means: add 0.002 * weight_value² to the total loss per weight



L2 is more common in practice — also called “weight decay”
L1 produces sparse models (some weights become exactly 0) — useful for feature selection
The penalty coefficient is a hyperparameter to tune
Particularly effective for smaller models

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Regularization: Dropout
Randomly zero out a fraction of layer outputs during training:
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.Dense(16, activation="relu"),
    layers.Dropout(0.5),
    layers.Dense(16, activation="relu"),
    layers.Dropout(0.5),
    layers.Dense(1, activation="sigmoid")
])

Typical rates: 0.2 to 0.5
At test time: no dropout, outputs scaled by (1 − rate)
Prevents “conspiracies” — accidental patterns that don’t generalize



Intuition: forces each neuron to be useful on its own, not rely on specific partners
Like training an ensemble of sub-networks that share weights
Dropout is one of the most effective and widely used techniques
Only applied during training — model.evaluate() and model.predict() don’t use it
NOTEBOOK DEMO: Continue in notebooks/chapter05_demo.ipynb, Demo 3. Applies dropout (0.8!) to the large 512-unit model from Demo 2. Plots all three: large without dropout, large with dropout, and original baseline. Shows that enough dropout tames even a massively oversized model to match the small baseline — a striking demonstration of how powerful dropout is.

title: Fundamentals of machine learning
text:
Discussion questions

Why does adding random noise features hurt generalization even though the model could just ignore them?
When would you choose K-fold over simple hold-out validation?
Should you always prefer a smaller model, or are there cases where bigger is better?



Noise features: the model finds spurious correlations — it can’t tell signal from noise
K-fold: small datasets, high-stakes decisions, when variance in results matters
Bigger models + strong regularization can outperform smaller ones (modern trend)

title: What is deep learning?
text:
Learning objectives

Distinguish between AI, machine learning, and deep learning
Identify the core components: weights, loss function, optimizer
Discuss the current state and realistic expectations for AI

title: What is deep learning?
text:
The nested circles

Nested circles labeled AI, ML, and DL.
AI: Effort to automate intellectual tasks (includes non-learning approaches)
ML: Learning rules from data instead of programming them
DL: Learning successive layers of representations



Symbolic AI dominated 1950s-1980s (expert systems, chess)
Failed on “fuzzy” problems: images, speech, language
Key question: Where does each approach make sense today?

title: What is deep learning?
text:
Classical programming vs ML

Classical programming vs ML workflow diagram.
Classical: Human writes rules → Computer applies rules
ML: Computer observes data + answers → Computer learns rules



“Trained rather than explicitly programmed”
What kinds of problems are still better suited to classical programming?

title: What is deep learning?
text:
ML vs statistical inference









Inference
ML




Goal
Estimate parameters + uncertainty
Predictions/generalization


Models
Theory-driven, causal
Flexible, data-driven


Data regime
Small n, strong assumptions
Large n, weaker assumptions





Goal: Inference cares about parameter values — “What is β? Is it significantly different from 0? What’s the 95% CI?” ML doesn’t care about parameters (millions of meaningless weights) — only whether predictions are accurate on held-out data.
Models: Inference builds models from domain knowledge (e.g., linear model because theory says X causes Y). ML uses flexible models (neural nets, trees) that learn structure from data.
Sweet spot: With small n, you need strong assumptions to learn anything. With large n, you can let the data speak.
Many modern methods blur the line (regularization, Bayesian ML, causal ML)

title: What is deep learning?
text:
The three ingredients of ML

Input data points — Images, text, audio, tabular data…
Example outputs — Labels, transcriptions, categories…
A way to measure success — How far off are we?

The third ingredient is crucial: the feedback signal drives learning.


This is where the term “supervised learning” comes from
What happens when you don’t have labeled data? (self-supervised, unsupervised)

title: What is deep learning?
text:
Representations

A representation is just a different way to look at/encode data that makes a task easier.
ML works by finding these useful representations automatically


Representation learning transforms data coordinates.
Separating black vs white becomes a simple rule: x > 0



ML = searching for useful representations + rules
“Hypothesis space” = the set of transformations we search through

title: What is deep learning?
text:
The “deep” in deep learning

Deep learning
Refers to successive layers of representations
Tens to hundreds of layers, all learned automatically


“A multistage information-distillation process”



The book is explicit: DL is not modeled on the brain; treat it as math
Shallow learning: 1-2 layers, often needed hand-crafted features
Neural networks: layers stacked, inspired (loosely!) by biology
The brain connection is overhyped — it’s really just math

title: What is deep learning?
text:
How it works: Three figures (1/3)
Weights parameterize each layer



Neural network layer with weights and parameters.



Learning = finding good values for millions of parameters

title: What is deep learning?
text:
How it works: Three figures (2/3)
Loss function measures quality



Loss function measuring prediction error.



Distance between predictions and targets
Also called: objective function, cost function

title: What is deep learning?
text:
How it works: Three figures (3/3)
Optimizer adjusts weights using the loss



Optimizer updates weights via backpropagation.



Backpropagation: the central algorithm
Training loop: repeat to reduce loss



Random initialization + repeated updates are what make the loop work

title: What is deep learning?
text:
What makes deep learning different?



Property
Why it matters




Simplicity
Automates feature engineering — no hand-crafting


Scalability
GPUs + batching enable much larger datasets


Versatility
Models are reusable, can do online learning





Foundation models: train once, use everywhere
Transfer learning changed the economics of ML
End-to-end DL replaces multistage pipelines that needed hand-crafted features

title: What is deep learning?
text:
The age of generative AI

Self-supervised learning: targets come from the input itself

Predict next word, reconstruct noisy images

ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Midjourney…
Foundation models: huge, trained on massive unlabeled data


These models work as a “fuzzy database of human knowledge”



Generative AI ideas date back to the 1990s
The first edition of this book (2017) had a “Generative AI” chapter!
Foundation models learn by reconstructing inputs (next-word, denoising)
Scale note: hundreds of billions of params, >1PB data, tens of $M to train

title: What is deep learning?
text:
What DL has achieved

Fluent chatbots & programming assistants (ChatGPT, Gemini, GitHub Copilot)
Photorealistic image and video generation
Human-level: image classification, speech/handwriting transcription
Dramatically improved translation & text-to-speech
Autonomous driving deployed in Phoenix, San Francisco, Los Angeles, and Austin (as of 2025)
Superhuman game playing (Go, Chess, Poker)



Discussion: Which of these surprised you most?
Which still has the most room to grow?

title: What is deep learning?
text:
Beware the hype
2023 predictions that didn’t pan out:

“No one needs to work anymore!”
10x-100x productivity gains imminent
Mass unemployment within a year

Reality (mid-2025):

“Tens of billions” in generative AI revenue (overall)
AI investment surpasses $100B annually, revenue closer to $10B



AGI predictions: Minsky (1967), OpenAI’s 2016 pitch, now again in 2023
Pattern: Overpromise → disappointment → “AI winter”?

title: What is deep learning?
text:
AGI soon?

AI: “Cognitive automation” = handles only what it’s trained for
Intelligence: “Cognitive autonomy” = facing the unknown, adapting, learning from it


“Think of it this way: AI is like a cartoon character, while intelligence is like a living being. A cartoon, no matter how realistic, can only act out the scenes it was drawn for.”



This is Chollet’s key argument against AGI hype
2013–2015: near-term AGI fear helped motivate OpenAI’s founding (2015)
OpenAI’s 2016 recruiting pitch: AGI by 2020
Discussion: Do you agree? Can scaling solve this?

title: What is deep learning?
text:
AI Winters: A history



Era
Hype
Crash




1960s
Symbolic AI → AGI in “a generation”
1970s: First AI winter


1980s
Expert systems boom
Early 1990s: Second AI winter


2020s
Generative AI, AGI soon?
???




Author’s view: Unlikely to be a full retreat, but some “air” will come out



Minsky 1967: AGI within a generation
Minsky 1970: General intelligence in 3-8 years
OpenAI 2016: AGI by 2020

title: What is deep learning?
text:
The long-term promise
From the previous edition of this book (written in 2017):

“AI will be your assistant… help educate your kids… watch over your health… be your interface to an increasingly complex world.”

2025 reality:

Well… we know how that turned out so far!
… but maybe we’re still “internet in 1995”

title: What is deep learning?
text:
Discussion questions

Is self-supervised learning fundamentally different from supervised?
What’s one thing DL shouldn’t be applied to?
Are we in another hype cycle? What’s different this time?



These are meant to spark discussion, not have right answers!

title: Club meetings
text:
Book club meetings

Volunteer leads discussion of a chapter

This is the best way to learn the material.

Presentations:

Review of material
Questions you have
Maybe live demo (Google Colab)

title: Club meetings
text:
How to edit the slides:

More info about editing: this github repo
Recorded, available on the Data Science Learning Community YouTube Channel (DSLC.video)

title: Club meetings
text:
Pace

Goal: 1 chapter/week
Ok to split overwhelming chapters
Ok to combine short chapters
Meet every week except holidays, etc

We will meet even if scheduled presenter unavailable

Slack is good place for offline discussion/troubleshooting

title: Club meetings
text:
Learning objectives (LOs)

Students who study with LOs in mind retain more
Tips:

Think “After today’s session, you will be able to {LO}”
Very roughly 1 per heading

title: Club meetings
text:
Group introductions

If you feel comfortable sharing:

Who are you?
Where you calling in from? (If you’re not comfortable sharing, skip)
How long have you been using Python?
What was your introduction to Python?
What are you most looking forward to during the club?

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Learning objectives

Explain what tensors are and their key attributes
Describe common tensor operations (element-wise, broadcasting, dot product)
Trace the gradient descent training loop step by step

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
A first look: MNIST

Sample MNIST handwritten digits.
60,000 training images, 10,000 test images
Each image: 28×28 grayscale pixels
Goal: Classify digits 0-9



Classic “hello world” of deep learning
Simple enough to train on a laptop
Complex enough to demonstrate real learning

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
The MNIST model in Keras
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.Dense(512, activation="relu"),
    layers.Dense(10, activation="softmax")
])
model.compile(optimizer="rmsprop",
              loss="sparse_categorical_crossentropy",
              metrics=["accuracy"])
model.fit(train_images, train_labels, epochs=5, batch_size=128)

Two Dense layers: fully connected neurons
ReLU: max(0, x) — introduces nonlinearity
Softmax: outputs probabilities for each class



We’ll understand every piece by end of chapter
Dense = every input connected to every output
512 neurons is arbitrary — a hyperparameter to tune

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
What is a tensor?



Rank
Name
Example




0
Scalar
12.5 — a single number


1
Vector
[1, 2, 3] — a 1D array


2
Matrix
2D array (rows × columns)


3+
Tensor
3D, 4D, 5D arrays…



Three key attributes:

Rank (ndim): Number of axes
Shape: Size along each axis, e.g. (60000, 28, 28)
Dtype: Data type (float32, int64, etc.)



In NumPy: x.ndim, x.shape, x.dtype
“Tensor” is just the general term for n-dimensional arrays
MNIST training data: rank-3 tensor, shape (60000, 28, 28)

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Real-world data as tensors








Data type
Shape
Example




Vector data
(samples, features)
Tabular data, embeddings


Timeseries
(samples, timesteps, features)
Stock prices, sensor data


Images
(samples, height, width, channels)
Photos (3 channels = RGB)


Video
(samples, frames, height, width, channels)
Movie clips





The first axis is almost always the “samples” or “batch” axis
Channels-last is default in TensorFlow/Keras
PyTorch uses channels-first: (samples, channels, height, width)

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Timeseries data tensor



A timeseries batch as a 3D tensor.



Shape: (samples, timesteps, features)
Example: 250 days of stock data with 3 features each

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Image data tensor



An image batch as a 4D tensor.



Shape: (samples, height, width, channels)
MNIST: (60000, 28, 28, 1) — grayscale, so 1 channel

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Tensor operations: Element-wise
# Element-wise addition
z = x + y  # Each element added independently

# ReLU activation
z = np.maximum(x, 0)  # Applied to each element

Operations applied independently to each element
Same shape in, same shape out
Examples: addition, subtraction, multiplication, ReLU



ReLU = Rectified Linear Unit
These are “embarrassingly parallel” — perfect for GPUs
Element-wise is the simplest tensor operation

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Tensor operations: Broadcasting
When shapes don’t match, the smaller tensor is broadcast to fit:
x = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])  # Shape (2, 2)
y = np.array([10, 20])          # Shape (2,)
z = x + y                        # y broadcast to (2, 2)
# Result: [[11, 22], [13, 24]]
Rules:

Axes are compared right-to-left
Dimensions must be equal OR one of them is 1
Missing axes are treated as 1



Broadcasting avoids explicit loops and copies
Very efficient — no new memory allocated
Common pitfall: broadcasting can hide shape bugs

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Tensor operations: Dot product



Matrix dot product computation.



The core operation in neural networks
Rows of first matrix × columns of second
Result shape: (a, b) @ (b, c) → (a, c)



Also called matrix multiplication, matmul, gemm
This is what Dense layers do: output = dot(input, W) + b
GPUs are optimized specifically for this operation

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Tensor reshaping
x = np.array([[0, 1], [2, 3], [4, 5]])  # Shape (3, 2)
x = x.reshape((6,))    # Shape (6,)
x = x.reshape((2, 3))  # Shape (2, 3)

Changes tensor shape without changing data
Total elements must remain the same
Transpose: special case that swaps axes



Reshaping is just reinterpreting the same memory
Zero-cost operation (no data copying)
Common use: flatten images before Dense layers

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Geometric interpretation



Dense layer as affine transform with ReLU.



Dense layers = affine transformations (rotation + translation)
ReLU = “creasing” that introduces nonlinearity
Stacking layers = composing transformations



Without nonlinearity, stacked layers collapse to a single linear transform
ReLU “folds” the space, creating piecewise linear boundaries
This is why depth matters: more folds = more complex boundaries

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
The paper ball analogy



Uncrumpling a crumpled paper ball.



“A deep network is a mathematical machine for uncrumpling complicated manifolds”



Imagine your data classes as tangled, crumpled paper
The network learns to “uncrumple” it until classes are separable
Each layer does part of the uncrumpling

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
The training loop

Draw a batch of training samples
Forward pass: Compute predictions
Compute loss: Measure prediction error
Backward pass: Calculate gradients
Update weights: Move opposite to gradient

Repeat until loss is low enough (or patience runs out).


“Epoch” = one pass through entire training set
“Batch” = subset of samples processed together
This loop is the same for almost all neural networks

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
What is a gradient?

Derivative: How output changes when input changes slightly
Gradient: Vector of partial derivatives (one per input dimension)
Points toward steepest increase of the function

For loss function L with weights W:
\[\nabla_W L = \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}, \ldots \right)\]


We want to decrease loss, so we move opposite to gradient
“Gradient descent” = repeatedly stepping downhill
The gradient tells us both direction AND magnitude of steepest ascent

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Gradient descent visualized



Gradient descent on a 2D loss surface.



Start at random point
Compute gradient (slope)
Take small step opposite to gradient
Repeat



Learning rate controls step size
Too large: overshoot and oscillate
Too small: slow convergence

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
Why “stochastic”? We use random mini-batches, not full dataset.



Variant
Key idea




SGD
Update = learning_rate × gradient


Momentum
Accumulate velocity to escape local minima


RMSprop
Adapt learning rate per-parameter


Adam
Combines momentum + RMSprop





Mini-batch SGD is the standard in deep learning
“Stochastic” = randomness from batch sampling
Adam is a good default; RMSprop works well too

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Local vs global minima



Local minimum vs global minimum.



Local minimum: Low point in neighborhood
Global minimum: Lowest point overall
In practice: local minima aren’t usually a problem in high dimensions



High-dimensional loss surfaces have many saddle points
Momentum helps escape saddle points
Most “local minima” in deep learning are nearly as good as global

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Backpropagation



Backward pass through computation graph.



Chain rule applied to computation graphs
Gradients flow backward from loss to parameters
Automatic differentiation: frameworks compute this for you



“Backprop” = efficient algorithm to compute all gradients
Key insight: reuse intermediate gradients (dynamic programming)
TensorFlow’s GradientTape, PyTorch’s autograd do this automatically

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Backprop: The chain rule
For composed functions: \(f(g(x))\)
\[\frac{d}{dx} f(g(x)) = \frac{df}{dg} \cdot \frac{dg}{dx}\]

Neural networks are long chains of functions
Gradient at any layer = gradient above × local gradient
Computed efficiently in one backward pass



This is calculus 101, but applied at massive scale
Modern frameworks trace operations and build the graph automatically
The “backward” pass mirrors the structure of the forward pass

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Looking back at MNIST
model.fit(train_images, train_labels, epochs=5, batch_size=128)
Now we understand what happens:

Batch of 128 images drawn from training data
Forward: Input → Dense(512, relu) → Dense(10, softmax) → Predictions
Loss: Compare predictions to labels (cross-entropy)
Backward: Compute gradients for all ~400K parameters
Update: RMSprop adjusts each weight
Repeat ~469 times per epoch (60000 ÷ 128)



5 epochs × 469 batches = ~2,345 weight updates
Each update uses gradients from 128 samples
Final accuracy: ~98% on test set

title: The mathematical building blocks of neural networks
text:
Discussion questions

Why do we use mini-batches instead of the full dataset?
What happens if the learning rate is too high? Too low?
Why are GPUs so effective for neural network training?



Mini-batches: memory limits, faster updates, regularization effect
Learning rate: too high = diverge, too low = slow/stuck
GPUs: massive parallelism for matrix operations

title: Image classification
text:
Learning objectives

Understand how convolutional networks match the structure of image data
Learn how data augmentation helps mitigate overfitting.
Learn to use pretrained models for small data problem (the usual case!)

title: Image classification
text:
Why this chapter matters

Computer vision was an early showcase for deep learning (competitions through the 2010s helped establish CNNs)
Convolutional neural networks (ConvNets / CNNs) remain the default backbone for many vision pipelines
CNN’s are a good first example of how to design architectures with inductive biases that match the data structure
Most practitioners face small or medium labeled sets, not web-scale data — the chapter centers that reality



Ask the group where they already use vision models (phones, search, medical imaging, etc.).

title: Image classification
section: Dense layers vs convolutions on images
text:
Dense layers vs convolutions on images

Dense layers learn global relationships over the whole flattened input
Conv layers learn local patterns in small spatial windows, reused everywhere on the grid
Two consequences the book stresses: translation invariance (same motif anywhere on the image) and a spatial hierarchy (edges → textures → parts → objects)




Spatial hierarchy of patterns from the book’s Figure 8.2.

title: Image classification
text:
Convolution in one pass

A kernel slides over the height–width grid; each location produces one vector of filter responses by dotting the kernel with the local patch of pixels
Output depth = number of filters; each channel is a feature map (where that filter fires)
Padding (valid vs same): control border shrinkage vs keeping spatial size
Stride > 1: skip positions and downsample (common in other architectures; classification stacks often prefer pooling instead)
Excellent you-tube video: Statquest

title: Image classification
text:
Max pooling

Fixed max over small windows (often 2×2, stride 2) — halves spatial size per axis, no learned weights inside the pool
Downsamples aggressively so deeper layers see broader context without exploding parameters
The book contrasts removing pooling: spatial maps stay large and the parameter burden grows

title: Image classification
text:
Understanding Conv2D parameter count

filters * (kernel_size^2 * input_channels + 1) (the +1 is for the bias term)
For example, the first Conv2D layer has 32 filters, a kernel size of 3x3, and 3 input channels (RGB):

32 * (3^2 * 3 + 1) = 32 * (27 + 1) = 32 * 28 = 896

title: Image classification
section: Cats and Dogs: train from scratch
text:
Cats and Dogs: train from scratch

Deep learning can still help with limited labels if you regularize well and use sensible preprocessing
Preprocessing (resize, scale, optional RGB vs grayscale) aligns inputs with how the network was designed to see the world
Expect modest accuracy from scratch on tiny sets — the chapter uses it to set up stronger tools next
About 78% test accuracy

title: Image classification
text:
Data augmentation

Random transforms at training time (flips, rotations, zooms, color jitter, etc.) artificially expand diversity
Acts as a strong regularizer: the network is pushed not to rely on exact pixel placements or lighting
Must stay plausible for the domain — extreme augmentation can introduce off-manifold examples
about 84% test accuracy with augmentation

title: Image classification
text:
Pretrained models: feature extraction



Reusing a convolutional base with a new classifier head (Figure 8.12).



Freeze a trained backbone; train only a small head on your labels
Fast, low data, good when your task is “close enough” to the pretraining distribution
Without augmentation 98% test accuracy; with augmentation about the same. (why?)

title: Image classification
text:
Pretrained models: Fine-tuning

Almost always combined with feature extraction to first train the new layers.
Unfreeze model weights and train with a smaller learning rate to adapt the pretrained features to your task.
More data and care needed; risk of overfitting or catastrophic forgetting if done too aggressively
Test accuracy about 98.6%


These techniques are great for small data, which is the case most of use will have to deal with! Discuss.

title: Image classification
text:
Discussion questions

Did you try the code in the chapter? What was your experience with training from scratch, augmentation, and pretrained models?
What are some examples of vision problems you might want to solve with small data?

For a fixed labeling budget, would you spend effort on more data, better augmentation, or a larger pretrained backbone — and in what order?
